speaker: interviewer
text: March 18, 1996. Survivor Rabbi Isaac Richards. Interviewer, [? Patty Gail Miskin ?]. City of Durban, country, South Africa, language, English. Rabbi, would you finish the story about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, Abraham--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Abraham Goldfinger?

speaker: interviewee
text: Abraham, then we-- I quickly got his things and put him in my truck. And we took him to Bergen-Belsen there to mingle and to lose himself. And-- and eventually, I learned that he-- he went on one of the ships and was caught and was imprisoned in Cyprus with-- together with all the thousands of others who were taken from the small ships. And of course, with the coming of-- of the establishment of the state of Israel, he went to live in Israel. He was appointed a chaplain to the Israeli army. He lived in Bnei Brak. And now, of course, he was very much more orthodox than he had been when he was in contact with me. And-- and-- and I visited him, oh, it's a long time ago. But I don't know whether he's still alive or not. He was a little older than me. And that, more or less, is the end of the story of-- of Goldfinger. But the-- what-- I was living, as I said, in-- in this alone in the early-- shortly after, I'd sort of come back into military life. And one day, a man marched into my office-- big, tall man-- clicked his heels, bowed, and said, Auerbach. I looked at him and I said, Richards. And he explained to me that he had come from Buchenwald. And he was going to Dusseldorf, which is not very far, about an hour's drive from my headquarters. And he was going to reestablish the community in Dusseldorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a survivor, Rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a survivor, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- had a title, doctor. What he was a doctor of, I don't-- I-- I don't know. But he was a wonderful organizer. And he went down to Dusseldorf. And indeed, he started to organize the people. And for one time, he was living with the oberburgermeister, sharing a flat with the oberburgermeister of Dusseldorf. And he had a lot of power. And he got a lovely room in what, I suppose, are one of the courts, the justice buildings, or something like that. Beautiful room. And he set it up as-- as a synagogue and started services. And I used to go there occasionally. And he organized the Jewish community, as far as he could, in Dusseldorf. Because there were a lot-- not a lot-- but there were quite a number of Jews who somehow had managed to hide, or because they had a-- a Jewish parent, or something like that, they managed to survive in Germany. And quite a few who came back from Theresienstadt, who'd survived Theresienstadt. And they now were living in Dusseldorf and, of course, in Cologne, which is another hour's drive from Dusseldorf. And one of the problems that these people had, that all civilians had, Jewish civilians in particular, was that for the six months or a year after the liberation, after the end of the war, no civilian post was allowed. No post to go out of Germany was allowed. And these people wanted to communicate with families or try and seek families in other countries out of-- outside Germany. And there was no way for them to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the very beginning, some of the chaplains had been helping, particularly with listing people, and so on and so forth. So he asked me, would I post some letters that people were sending to relations in England, in America, in South Africa? And I said, yes, certainly. So he brought me a little bundle of letters, and I put them through the military post, and off they went. But it started getting more and more. And of course, then Lily would write to Chaim, and say, dear Chaim, and sign it, Lily. But when they came back, who was Chaim? I didn't know who they were. So when the post came back, I'd give it to him and he would try and sort it out.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he was being very Germanic in his way. He thought, this is not good enough. So he had a little sticker printed which said, please answer-- in your answer, address it to Chaplain Rabbi I. Richards, this-- this post box, and so on and so forth. So the letters started pouring in, and parcels started to arrive. And peas would fall out and all sorts of things. And I would take it all to him and leave it there. This went on for a couple of months, because by that time, he's not only enrolled Dusseldorf and Unna and Hamm, and all the other little places, which had got little communities, but he'd also enrolled Cologne, as well, which had quite a big community by then.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one fine day, I get a phone call from Major So-and-So from the Army postal officer. Could he come and see me? I said, oy vey. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, OK, come and see me. So I called [? Abie ?] quickly. I said, get the van and get all these parcels and everything out of here as quick as you can. You go driving around, you see? And-- and about an hour later, this officer came to see me. And I explained to him. I said, yes, I am doing something wrong in the eyes of the law, perhaps, but this is a humanitarian thing. It's the Jewish people have suffered enough, one thing and another. So he was very understanding and he said, OK, [? padre, ?] but stop it, which I did. And fortunately, not long after, postal facilities--

speaker: interviewer
text: Resumed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --resumed. Now, it was very interesting. This man, Auerbach, as I said, he was sort of recognized by the British authorities as being the head of the Jewish community of the area. And he had lots of facilities granted to him. But he overdid it. He wanted-- he asked me if I would join-- bring my soldiers and join in the Seder. That would've-- would've been early in--

speaker: interviewer
text: April.

speaker: interviewee
text: '46. I'm sorry, yes, quite right. Early in '46. Would I join with him, bring my soldiers, and have a Seder? I thought it over. I said no, it won't mix. I get supplies and everything else from the army, and there might be some funny business. No, leave it alone. And I had-- I held the Seder that-- that-- in 1946 in April at a beautiful place on the Mohnesee. You saw the film. What was it called?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a holiday camp for officers, for Nazi officers, on that dam. And we held our Passover Seder there. And-- and he had his Passover Seder. I believe he had champagne, what he didn't have, and so on. But he got a little bit over-confident. And he went-- he did take too many liberties. And the brigadier in charge of the whole area, the brigadier said he didn't want him anymore as the representative of the Jews. He gave him the sack, in other words. Now, the community, and that we'd already formed-- they had already formed a-- a-- an organization of the Jews in Germany, which lasted for many, many years. And they looked to-- to Auerbach, and they were rather upset. So I went to the brigadier. And I first had an interview with him myself. And I could see that he had certain justification. The man was just being too bossy and thought he could just do exactly as he wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, look, you appoint-- you tell me who you want. But I'm not having this cheeky [? be ?] Auerbach. And then I said, well, could you receive a delegation from the community? And he said, yes, I'll do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so, I really felt I was part of history. I brought in this delegation of Jewish leaders of the area into this beautiful chamber in which he-- he was holding court, as it were. And of course, many of his officers were sitting with him, and translators, and so on. And they presented their case for-- for Auerbach. And one of the things that had riled him more than anything else was that Auerbach had said, if he tries to interfere with what I'm doing, I'll go back to the American zone. And I'll work there.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And he thought this was, you know, the height of cheek after all that he'd-- he had done a lot. The brigadier had been very good in his ways. And so the-- here I introduced the delegation. I'm afraid it was fruitless. But he did go to the American zone. And he-- he had a knock-- you know, he did get some position. But there was an awful lot of things which were, perhaps, not in order. And he faded into the background. In fact, he committed suicide. So he had a tragic end. But he did-- he did serve the communities in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did a lot of good in the course.

speaker: interviewee
text: He did a lot of good, but in the end, he blotted his copybook.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi, while you were in Germany, you met Rabbi Weiler, didn't you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was going to be-- he was instrumental in what happened in your future life.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us a little bit about that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, well, I-- I as you-- as I mentioned at the beginning, I had served two small congregations, orthodox congregations. And they had cured me of wanting to be a rabbi in an orthodox congregation. And I had determined that I am not going back into the ministry at all. What I was going to do, I didn't know. And then there was an ad ran in the saying that Rabbi Weiler would be in London. And he was looking for an assistant. Would I contacted him there?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I contacted-- so I thought, well, what's this all about? Because I knew little or nothing about progressive Judaism. Practically nothing. In fact, I might even have been prejudiced against it from my orthodox upbringing. So I wrote him a letter to ask him what-- what his point of view was, what it was all about. I don't think I ever really got a proper answer from him. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But he said, could he if he came to Germany, could he-- could I show him around the camps and such like things? Because no civilians were allowed into Germany even at that time. This was mid-19--

speaker: interviewee
text: '46. No civilians could come and no sightseers. So I replied and said, yes, I could do that. Because I had my own-- you know, I-- I had-- I arranged own itinerary. I wasn't really--

speaker: interviewee
text: Under anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Subject to anybody as long as I did my job and so on. And so I said, yes, I could do that. And I waited to hear from him. And then I heard from him that he couldn't get a permit. Would I meet him in Paris? I thought, oh, that's fine. So I went to the major and I said, could I-- I showed him, you know, what it was. And they were anxious to let officers and men who had somewhere to go to go-- get out. So he said, yes, OK. And he gave me a pass to go to Paris, all-- all expenses paid.

speaker: interviewee
text: But lo and behold, one fine day before I would-- was to set out, I got a phone call from somewhere deep in the American zone from Weiler. He'd managed to get into Germany, and could I meet him at such and such a place? And to cut a long story short, eventually he came eight hours late. But anyway, eventually I met him. And I took him mainly to Berlin. Because, as I mentioned earlier, thousands of Jews were now coming through Berlin from further deep into Eastern Europe, from deep into Russia to try and get out. Get away from the Russians.

speaker: interviewee
text: And to get on the train-- gravy train to Israel. And so we went. So I took him there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: [DRINKS] And from morning till evening, he was on the go. And my faith [? with ?] [? Abie, ?] he said, you know, rabbi, if you got with that guy, he'll kill you. [LAUGHTER] Because I'm not as energetic as he was. And we met with various leaders, particularly American Joint Distribution Committee. And in one particular camp where there were a couple of thousand Jews from Russia, he got up on a balcony and called a meeting. And he addressed them. He was a wonderful speaker, and he could speak in Yiddish, Hebrew, French, whatever language you wanted. I don't know about French, but he spoke French fairly well, too. He even spoke Zulu a little bit. And he addressed them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went around for about three or four days together to the various camps. And it ended up by him asking me would I come and join him in South Africa? I tried-- I asked him what his standpoint was. And it was-- you know, when I found it was not too-- too left-wing reformed, I decided that this might be a good idea.

speaker: interviewee
text: I consulted with my late wife, Lily [INAUDIBLE], my first wife. And-- and she was quite willing, despite the fact that she came from an even more orthodox background than I did. But she saw that this might be my future. And so I-- I said yes. And shortly after Yom Kippur in 1946, I received a cable from South Africa offering me a post as assistant to Weiler and to be in charge of his Hebrew classes, his cheder, and so on. And this decided me. I had been offered a-- an orthodox post in Australia. But I knew a lot about South Africa because I had an uncle and I had cousins here. And I'd met these cousins who had visited us in England. Some of them had been with us in English. In fact, in the early 1920s, they were caught in Russia during the First World War. And in the early 1920s-- I think it was about '21-- they came from Russia to Liverpool on their way to South Africa to join their father. And-- and so we knew them, and we corresponded with them. And I also had an uncle who'd lived in South Africa and come back to England. And he used to tell us how wonderful it was. So I chose--

speaker: interviewer
text: South Africa.

speaker: interviewee
text: --progressive Judaism in South Africa in place of orthodoxy and Brisbane in Australia. And I can't say that I ever really had any regrets. I think it's been most fulfilling.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you actually leave Germany? When did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I left--

speaker: interviewer
text: --finish your work in the camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well I'd-- I'd left the the camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I was-- civilian work. I left late '46. It must've been about early in November. It was early in November. Because what happened was I went on leave after the high holidays and Sukkot. I went on leave to London in England. And I went into the war office to the department of [INAUDIBLE], us chaplains. And I asked if I could get a-- what was called a Class B release because I still had about two or three months go. Oh, he said, yes, old boy. Certainly. Said, I'll tell you what I'll do. He says, I'll tell you what I'll do. I'll transfer you from your present station to the London command. And you don't need to go back. And you'll be out in 10 days. Good heavens, this is no good because I had all my goodies, and things, and a few things that I'd accumulated in Luneburg it was at the time. And I thought to leave that to the-- to the mercy of my [INAUDIBLE] man and other people, there's nothing doing. But I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then I went up to-- to Liverpool, to-- to see my folk. And while I was there, apparently on Friday night-- it was a Saturday-- a telegram came from the war office saying that I was released. But my mother-in-law, God rest her soul, was very religious. And she wouldn't even open it or wouldn't telephone me. So immediately-- immediately on Saturday, my leave was up. I got on the-- on the train. I went to-- to Halle, I think, and got a boat back to Germany. And I did a quick tour to say goodbye to all the people I knew and one thing and another. We even went up to Berlin to say goodbye to some people that I'd been working with there. And I remember it was November, because on the way back, warmly dressed as I was, it was freezing. And as the-- as the rain was coming down, it was freezing on the-- on the wind screens. And-- and I got back to my station in Luneburg. And I went in to see the major. And he said, hey, what are you doing here? You're-- you're demobilized. And I said, thank you very much. Bye-bye.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took the next plane out. And I went-- then you-- you you came to London, and you went to someplace somewhere in the center of London. And they fitted you out with a suit, and a shirt, and a tie, and shoes, and a hat. [LAUGHTER] And this was my new uniform and I walked out looking like a not-so-smart civilian. And then I waited some time while I got-- to get a plane out to South Africa. And I landed in South Africa on the 16th of December, 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you became-- you once-- you immediately went into the rabbinate in Johannesburg.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And I-- yes, and I assisted-- I practically sort of was-- did most of the pastoral work while Weiler, who was a great politician, and traveler, and so on. He was looked after the-- that side of affairs in the main.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rabbi, how would you say your experiences in Germany and the Holocaust influenced your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I don't think they did really except in that they made me grow up. Because as an orthodox minister, one lives or very hedged-in life. And I don't think it was just the Holocaust, but it was the army and working with people that caused me to see that there were certain things which just didn't work anymore, and had to be altered, and had to be changed. And as I say, I think in the main, it developed me and helped me to grow up as a real, mature person and understand other people's problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: It certainly helped you as a rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And final question. What message do you believe that we should learn from the Holocaust? And in particularly, for young people, what message would you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The message in one word is tolerance. Understanding that there are other points of view, understanding that one must respect even one-- even if one cannot accept. One must respect other people's points of view. And putting away prejudice, not-- not allowing prejudice to enter into one's life and into one's way of thinking. And this is something, which of course, South Africa above all requires. We've got too many people who are fixed in their ways and everybody else is wrong. Everybody is right and everybody is wrong. We must give everybody the right, within obvious limits, the right to follow their faith, their philosophy of life, their culture, their language, and encourage this difference. I believe that the idea of the rainbow people is a very, very valuable thought. And as you know, the rainbow in the Bible was the symbol of peace. God said, the rainbow shall be a symbol [INAUDIBLE] it as a sign that never again will mankind be destroyed. So let us look to the rainbow and see we don't destroy the wonderful land that which we have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, Rabbi.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I was caught up early in 1943. I think it was March or April. And I reported to Chester, which is a-- also an ancient historic town. And then together with all the other chaplains who had been called up at the same time, went on a three-week course to familiarize ourselves with military rigmarole and-- and so on. We didn't do any actual square bashing, but we had to study map reading and such like things. And I was the only Jewish person, Jewish chaplain at this course, which lasted for three weeks. Then we were ready to go out into the field. If you look at the photograph, I am on the second row, the second person on the left. In those days, we wore what we call, dog collars, to show exactly who we were. And it was very important because it gave us entry to places where we might be questioned before we could get into see the boys and so on. And so that is what this is about. The class of 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a photograph-- an informal one, I should say-- of myself in uniform. Informal because one was supposed to be wearing one's hat. And that's the only photograph, really, that I have of that period. I don't know where all the others have disappeared to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's get a little bit of background to who he was and [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the autograph of Otto Frank, the father of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Anne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of Anne, of the well-known diaries of Anne Frank. And this is well after the war. I met him at a conference in Holland some 20-odd years ago. And this is his own autobiography. He is a very active-- or was. He is no longer with us. Very active member of the progressive Jewish community in Amsterdam.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt was a camp to which most of the German and Czech Jews were sent. It was, I think, in Czechoslovakia. And the Germans sort of set it up-- pretended it was a model camp. Although there was just as much suffering there but not in the same way. And they brought the Red Cross to see it, and they even had an orchestra and a well known film about it. And they printed the money for the camp inmates only. And this is a complete set of that money from-- ranging from 100, I think, down to one. It says, receipt for 50 kroners, the-- the order of the Jews in Theresienstadt. And it even gives his name on some of them. There's his signature. Jacob something. Jacob Edelstein.

speaker: interviewer
text: The medals.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I claim no special distinctions. These are more or less the standard medals which all who served in the British Army, and including service in Europe, which we all more or less got. There's no VCs or anything like that amongst them.
